Summary:
Systematic elaboration.

RUNX2 is a master transcription factor in osteoblasts required for osteoblast differentiation and bone remodeling and for regulation of XCR1 expression in MG-63 osteoblast cells. RUNX2 contains an intrinsically disordered region (IDR) that undergoes phase separation to form condensates involved in transcriptional regulation in osteoblasts. RUNX2‑IDR phase separation mediates long‑range regulation between the rs4683184 enhancer and the XCR1 promoter. In OE‑IDR MG‑63 osteoblasts increased RUNX2 condensates co‑localized with rs4683184, the XCR1 promoter, and nascent XCR1 RNA. This co‑localization was associated with increased XCR1 mRNA and protein while RUNX2 expression remained unchanged. In primary human osteoblasts (A/G) and MG‑63 cells (A/A), RUNX2 preferentially binds the rs4683184‑A allele and this binding increases enhancer activity of the rs4683184‑A region. RUNX2 knockdown reduces XCR1 and RUNX2 overexpression elevates XCR1 mRNA and protein.
Reference: Zhang, Y., Li, X. H., Peng, P., Qiu, Z. H., Di, C. X., Chen, X. F., Wang, N. N., Chen, F., He, Y. W., Liu, Z. B., Zhao, F., Zhu, D. L., Dong, S. S., Hu, S. Y., Yang, Z., Li, Y. P., Guo, Y., & Yang, T. L. (2025). RUNX2 Phase Separation Mediates Long-Range Regulation Between Osteoporosis-Susceptibility Variant and XCR1 to Promote Osteoblast Differentiation. Adv Sci (Weinh), 12(6), e2413561. https://doi.org/10.1002/advs.202413561

Summary: RUNX2 is a well-known master regulator of osteoblast differentiation in humans and is critical for modulating chondrocyte-to-osteoblast differentiation and for regulating chondrocyte hypertrophy. RUNX2 modulates transcriptional activation and multiple signaling pathways. RUNX2 is involved in osteogenesis and in the maturation of chondrocytes. A FOS-RUNX2 gene fusion was reported in osteoblastoma samples. The fusion removed the C-terminal part of FOS that is involved in FOS degradation and deleted the 3′-untranslated region of FOS mRNA required for its interaction with miRNA.
Reference: Lin, T. C. (2023). RUNX2 and Cancer. Int J Mol Sci, 24(8), 7001. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms24087001

Summary: Runx2 (Cbfa1) is a master switch for osteoblast differentiation from mesenchymal progenitor cells. Osteoblasts can express Runx2. In compressed periodontal tissue, bone morphogenic proteins and Runx2 expression are induced, and these factors potentiate osteoblast differentiation and bone mineralization.
Reference: Li, Y., Jacox, L. A., Little, S. H., & Ko, C. C. (2018). Orthodontic tooth movement: The biology and clinical implications. Kaohsiung J Med Sci, 34(4), 207-214. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.kjms.2018.01.007

Summary: Runx2 is the first transcription factor that directs multipotent mesenchymal cells to osteoblast-lineage cells. The expression of Ihh in prehypertrophic chondrocytes is required for Runx2 expression in perichondrial mesenchymal cells during endochondral ossification. Runx2 induces Sp7 and converts Runx2+ osteoprogenitors into Runx2+Sp7+ preosteoblasts. Canonical Wnt signaling is necessary for Runx2+Sp7+ preosteoblasts to differentiate into immature osteoblasts expressing Col1a1 and Spp1. Runx2 is expressed in all osteoblast-lineage cells and is critical for osteoblast differentiation; germline deletion of Runx2 results in a complete lack of intramembranous and endochondral bone. In suture mesenchymal cells and in calvarial development Runx2 in osteoblasts directly induces Hedgehog, Fgf, Wnt, and Pthlh pathway genes and the transcription factor Dlx5. Activation of Hedgehog, Fgf, Wnt, and Pthlh signaling pathways is required for the proliferation of suture mesenchymal cells. Activation of Hedgehog, Fgf, Wnt, and Pthlh signaling pathways is required for the commitment of suture mesenchymal cells to osteoblast-lineage cells, and those pathways and Dlx5 reciprocally regulate Runx2. Fgf signaling activates and stabilizes Runx2 through Mapk1/3 phosphorylation. Runx family transcription factors are stabilized and have enhanced DNA-binding via heterodimer formation with Cbfb. An osteoblast-specific Runx2 enhancer is activated by direct binding of Dlx5/6 and Mef2c and by indirect binding of Tcf7/Ctnnb1, Smad1, Sp7, and Sox5/Smad6; those factors form an enhanceosome. More than half the dosage of Runx2 is necessary for induction of these genes in suture mesenchymal cells, whereas half the dosage suffices in committed osteoblasts and Runx2+/- osteoblasts normally express bone matrix protein genes. Runx2 and Sp7 are required for immature osteoblasts to mature and express Bglap/Bglap2 and high Col1a1.
Reference: Komori, T. (2024). Regulation of Skeletal Development and Maintenance by Runx2 and Sp7. Int J Mol Sci, 25(18), 10102. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms251810102

Summary: In multiple myeloma, RUNX2 expression is increased in myeloma cells and promotes myeloma-driven bone destruction in vitro and in vivo. RUNX2 upregulation in myeloma cells suppresses osteoblast activity and enhances osteoclast activity. In conditioned culture medium experiments, RUNX2 enhanced osteoclast activity. RUNX2 increased myeloma-driven suppression of osteoblast activity. RUNX2 facilitated osteoblast-mediated differentiation of osteoclasts. RUNX2 promotes bone resorption via regulation of TGFβ, MMP-9, ITGA5, or RANKL. Therapeutic inhibition of RUNX2 may protect against bone destruction by maintaining the balance between osteoblast and osteoclast activity in multiple myeloma.
Reference: Huang, B., Liu, H., Chan, S., Liu, J., Gu, J., Chen, M., Kuang, L., Li, X., Zhang, X., & Li, J. (2023). RUNX2 promotes the suppression of osteoblast function and enhancement of osteoclast activity by multiple myeloma cells. Med Oncol, 40(4), 115. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12032-023-01960-8
